forecasts.reasoning:
The resolution criteria state that the question resolves as 'Yes' if any of the following candidates (Gnangbo Kacou, Laurent Gbagbo, Pascal Affi N'Guessan, Jean-Louis Billon, or Simone Gbagbo) is removed from the electoral list or otherwise becomes legally ineligible to run in the 2025 presidential election of Ivory Coast before July 1, 2025. 

Laurent Gbagbo, the former President and the designated 2025 candidate for the PPA-CI party, is confirmed by multiple sources up to May 2025 to be legally ineligible due to a 20-year prison sentence. His name was removed from the electoral list in August 2024 and he was subsequently left off the provisional electoral list published in March 2025. Given that he fulfills the exact conditions of the resolution criteria and news reports natively describe him as "barred" and "removed from the race," this question has essentially already met the criteria for a YES resolution. Because the prompt instructs probabilities between 0.05 and 0.95, I am indicating the highest possible confidence at 0.95.

BC Hydro explicitly removed Tesla from its EV charger rebate program on March 12, 2025, to combat US tariffs and preference Canadian goods. As of late May 2025, the US-Canada trade war was still ongoing under new PM Mark Carney, although tensions were slightly de-escalating (with Canada pausing some counter-tariffs mid-month and a May 29 US court decision striking down parts of Trump's tariff agenda). Despite this de-escalation, reversing retaliatory policies at the provincial and administrative level usually takes time. Given the extremely short window (roughly one month) between these late-May developments and the July 1 resolution date, it is probable that BC Hydro will not update its eligibility lists fast enough to reinstate Tesla, especially since the initial ban was also characterized as politically targeting Elon Musk. Therefore, I estimate a 72% chance the exclusion remains on the BC Hydro website by July 2025.

To resolve YES, both remaining inmates (Derrick Groves and Antoine Massey) must be captured by July 1, 2025 (33 days from the latest May 29 update). 8 out of the 10 escapees were recaptured within 13 days, which highlights a very effective and high-resource law enforcement response, including multiple agencies, AI analysis of jail calls by the DA, and the arrest of at least 11 accomplices to dismantle their support networks. A reward of $50,000 for each remaining fugitive provides a massive incentive for public tips (which were already responsible for 3 previous captures). However, resolving YES requires catching *both* men, meaning there are two distinct points of failure. Massey is a known 'escape artist' who broke out of juvenile detention in 2007 and has experience being on the run. Additionally, authorities acknowledge they could be anywhere in the US at this point, and past the initial two-week 'hot' period, highly-motivated fugitives can occasionally go underground for months. If we estimate an 80% chance for each being captured in the next month, the combined probability of both being captured is about 64%. Therefore, 63% is a well-calibrated estimate.

As of late April 2025, Lebanon's Economy Minister indicated the country only "hopes" to reach a preliminary staff-level agreement (SLA) with the IMF "within months." Meanwhile, an IMF fact-finding mission in March 2025 noted that Lebanon still needs a comprehensive economic strategy. An SLA is a necessary first step that only 'paves the way' for formal loan negotiations and does not itself constitute the IMF agreeing to disburse funds. Actual funding requires the IMF Executive Board's approval, which is contingent upon Lebanon implementing complex prior actions (reforms). Given that an SLA is not even expected until later in the year, and historical precedents show these negotiations and reform implementations take many months or even years, it is virtually impossible for a final funding agreement to be announced before the July 1, 2025 deadline.

As of May 30, 2025, there is strong public discussion about an upcoming summit between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin to negotiate a ceasefire in the Ukraine war. However, the most heavily discussed and likely venue for this meeting is Saudi Arabia, not Russia. While Putin extended an invitation for Trump to visit Moscow, and Trump made offhand remarks earlier in the year about visiting, he explicitly denied French media reports that he would visit Russia in May. As of May 19, 2025, the Kremlin confirmed that no specific date or location had been agreed upon for a meeting. Given the massive logistical and security requirements for a US presidential visit to Moscow—an event that has not occurred since 2009—it is highly improbable that such a visit would be planned and executed within the remaining 6-week window before July 1, especially when neutral third countries are actively being used for these peace talks.

James Comey posted an ambiguous photo ('86 47') which generated political backlash and a voluntary one-hour interview with the Secret Service on May 16, 2025. He was not taken into custody, and he removed the post claiming he was unaware of any violent connotations. Legal and security experts cited by CNN consider a prosecution to be highly challenging and 'fruitless' because of the high bar for proving a true threat. Given that we are at the end of May 2025, there are only about 4 weeks left until the July 1 resolution date. Convening a grand jury and securing an indictment against a high-profile former official over an arguably innocuous social media post takes significant time, making an arrest within this short window extremely improbable. The probability is set to the minimum allowable threshold.
